title: What Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a Phase II archaeological evaluation is triggered during a federally funded or permitted project, the stakes rise considerably. Unlike the broad survey work of Phase I, Phase II is a focused investigation designed to answer one critical regulatory question: does this site meet the criteria for listing on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP)?

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted excavation study that determines whether an archaeological site qualifies for the National Register of Historic Places. Archaeologists conduct systematic testing—including shovel tests, test units, and artifact analysis—to assess a site's significance under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act.

Federal agencies, state departments of transportation, and private developers who encounter archaeological sites during Phase I surveys must understand what Phase II entails. This evaluation directly shapes project timelines, budgets, and legal obligations under the National Historic Preservation Act. Skipping or rushing this step can result in costly project delays, regulatory violations, and reputational damage.

Why Is Phase II Required Under Section 106?

Phase II is required when a Phase I survey identifies a site that may be eligible for the NRHP, and a federal nexus exists for the project. Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act mandates that federal agencies consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties, and that includes archaeological sites that might qualify.

The purpose is not to excavate everything—it is to gather enough data to make a defensible eligibility determination. The State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) and the lead federal agency must concur on whether a site is significant before the project can move forward. Without this determination, regulators have no way to assess project impacts, and agencies cannot fulfill their legal obligations.

In practical terms, Phase II findings either clear a site from further consideration or trigger Phase III data recovery, which is the most intensive and expensive form of archaeological work. Getting Phase II right protects all parties and keeps projects on schedule.

How Is a Phase II Evaluation Conducted?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation involves systematic, targeted excavation designed to characterize a site's horizontal and vertical extent, artifact assemblage, stratigraphic integrity, and research potential. The methodology is tailored to the specific site type—whether prehistoric, historic-era, or multi-component.

Common field methods include:

  • Test units (typically 1x1 or 1x2 meters) excavated in natural or arbitrary levels
  • Shovel test intervals reduced to increase spatial resolution around features
  • Feature documentation including hearths, post molds, storage pits, and foundations
  • Controlled artifact recovery with provenience tied to exact unit and level

Laboratory analysis follows field work. Artifacts are cleaned, catalogued, and analyzed by material class—ceramics, lithics, faunal remains, historic glass, metal, and more. Radiocarbon dating, phytolith analysis, or other specialist studies may be employed depending on site characteristics. The final technical report presents all findings and a formal NRHP eligibility recommendation.

What Are the NRHP Eligibility Criteria for Archaeological Sites?

An archaeological site must meet at least one of four National Register criteria to be considered eligible. Criterion D—the most commonly applied to archaeological sites—asks whether the property has yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important to history or prehistory.

Evaluating Criterion D requires demonstrating that the site retains sufficient integrity and research potential to address meaningful questions about the past. A site with intact stratigraphy, well-associated artifacts, and dateable features is far more likely to qualify than a disturbed scatter of isolated finds.

Other criteria can also apply. A site associated with significant historical events (Criterion A), important persons (Criterion B), or distinctive architectural characteristics (Criterion C, less common for buried sites) may also qualify. Archaeologists working through Phase II evaluations must consider all applicable criteria and clearly justify their eligibility recommendation in the report.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take?

The timeline for a Phase II archaeological evaluation depends on site size, complexity, landowner access, and laboratory backlog. Most projects require four to twelve weeks from field mobilization to final report delivery, though larger or more complex sites can extend well beyond that.

Field work itself may last only a few days for a small site, or several weeks for a multi-acre multi-component site. Laboratory analysis and report writing often take longer than the field component. Scheduling SHPO review—which typically adds several weeks—must also be factored into project planning.

Project managers and planners should build Phase II time into their schedules early. Waiting until ground-disturbing work is imminent creates pressure that can compromise thoroughness and lead to regulatory pushback. Early coordination with a qualified cultural resource management firm helps keep projects on track.

What Happens After a Phase II Evaluation?

After the Phase II report is submitted, the lead federal agency and SHPO review the findings and reach a formal determination of eligibility. If a site is found not eligible, it is typically cleared, and the project may proceed without further archaeological constraints in that area.

If a site is determined eligible, the project team must consult with the SHPO and other consulting parties to determine whether the undertaking will have an adverse effect on the resource. If adverse effects cannot be avoided, Phase III data recovery—a large-scale excavation to recover the site's research value before destruction—may be required as a mitigation measure.

In some cases, project redesign can avoid the eligible site entirely, eliminating the need for Phase III work. Early and transparent coordination using GIS and remote sensing tools during project planning can help identify these avoidance opportunities before commitments are made. TVAR works with clients from early planning through final regulatory clearance to achieve efficient, compliant outcomes.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site with potential NRHP eligibility and the project has a federal nexus—meaning federal funding, permitting, or agency involvement. The lead federal agency and SHPO must concur that Phase II testing is needed before work begins.

Who can conduct a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Phase II evaluations must be conducted by archaeologists who meet the Secretary of the Interior's Professional Qualification Standards—typically an M.A. or Ph.D. in archaeology plus supervised field experience. The Principal Investigator is responsible for study design, oversight, and the final eligibility determination submitted to the SHPO.

How much does a Phase II archaeological evaluation cost?

Costs vary widely based on site size, number of test units, laboratory requirements, and report complexity. Small evaluations may run $10,000–$25,000; larger or more complex studies can exceed $100,000. Early scoping with a qualified CRM firm helps project owners budget accurately and avoid mid-project surprises.

Can a Phase II evaluation be avoided?

Phase II can sometimes be avoided through project redesign that places all ground disturbance outside site boundaries, effectively avoiding the resource entirely. This requires early coordination between the project team, archaeologists, and the SHPO to confirm that avoidance is sufficient and that no adverse effect will occur.

Partnering With TVAR for Phase II Evaluations

A well-executed Phase II archaeological evaluation is one of the most consequential steps in any federally involved project. The eligibility determination it produces shapes every subsequent decision—from project design to budget to schedule. Cutting corners at this stage creates risk far more expensive than the evaluation itself.

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research brings RPA-certified archaeologists, deep regional knowledge of the Southeast, and a rigorous methodology to every Phase II evaluation we undertake. Our team coordinates closely with SHPO offices and federal agencies to produce clear, defensible reports that move your project forward efficiently.

If your project has identified potential archaeological sites and you need a trusted partner for Phase II evaluation, contact TVAR today to discuss your project needs and timeline.
excerpt: A Phase II archaeological evaluation determines if a site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places through systematic testing and artifact analysis.
